paragraphs.text:
Using only the SRD‑46 results retrieved so far, I **cannot yet identify a vetted violation** of the Irving–Williams series.

What the database search currently shows is that the relevant divalent ions **are present** in SRD‑46 for simple amino-acid ligands, especially glycine and alanine, but the returned summaries are still **coverage tables**, not matched single-condition log β values for the same stoichiometry across the full Mn–Fe–Co–Ni–Cu–Zn set. That means we do **not yet have the actual comparable constants needed to prove a reversal**.

paragraphs.text:
### What this means
| Conclusion | Basis |
|---|---|
| There are suitable ligand systems in SRD‑46 for testing Irving–Williams | Glycine and alanine both show all six divalent first-row ions in the retrieved stability summary |
| No exception is yet demonstrated | The output lists pair coverage and ranges, not directly comparable matched log K values under identical T, ionic strength, solvent, and stoichiometry |
| Any claim of a violation now would be premature | Irving–Williams can only be tested on like-for-like constants |

grounded_claims.evidence_snippet: Irving–Williams requires comparing like-for-like constants (same T, ionic strength, solvent, stoichiometry). This methodological point is general chemical knowledge, not a database row.

paragraphs.text:
### Most plausible exception types to look for next
If exceptions do appear after extracting matched values, the strongest mechanistic hypotheses would be:

| Possible exception pattern | Likely explanation |
|---|---|
| Cu²⁺ not the most stable | Jahn–Teller distortion or ligand geometry that disfavors tetragonally distorted Cu²⁺ coordination |
| Zn²⁺ > Cu²⁺ for a rigid ligand | Size/cavity match or preorganization favoring d¹⁰ Zn²⁺ over Cu²⁺ |
| Ni²⁺ unusually high | Strong-field N-donor ligands stabilizing Ni²⁺, sometimes with geometry/spin-state effects |
| Any scattered inversion in weak ligands | Non-matched conditions, protonation/speciation differences, or different reported stoichiometries |

claims.text: ### Most plausible exception types to look for next
If exceptions do appear after extracting matched values, the strongest mechanistic hypotheses would be:
| Cu²⁺ not the most stable | Jahn–Teller distortion or ligand geometry that disfavors tetragonally distorted Cu²⁺ coordination |
| Zn²⁺ > Cu²⁺ for a rigid ligand | Size/cavity match or preorganization favoring d¹⁰ Zn²⁺ over Cu²⁺ |
| Ni²⁺ unusually high | Strong-field N-donor ligands stabilizing Ni²⁺, sometimes with geometry/spin-state effects |
| Any scattered inversion in weak ligands | Non-matched conditions, protonation/speciation differences, or different reported stoichiometries |

Rationale that rigid/preorganized ligands can favor d10 Zn(II) over Cu(II) is domain knowledge; not present in tool outputs.
Statement that strong-field N-donor ligands can yield high Ni(II) stability is domain knowledge; not directly in database rows.
Explanation that inversions for weak ligands can arise from condition/speciation/stoichiometry differences is domain knowledge; not in tool rows.

paragraphs.text:
### Provisional interpretation
| Status | Statement |
|---|---|
| Verified exceptions from retrieved data | None yet |
| Best candidates for deeper checking | Glycine and alanine systems |
| Working hypothesis if exceptions are later found | They will most likely arise from **ligand-specific geometry/preorganization**, **Jahn–Teller effects for Cu²⁺**, or **comparison artifacts from unmatched conditions** rather than a true failure of Irving–Williams |

If you want, I can continue the SRD‑46 search and pull the **actual matched log β values** for glycine and alanine to test whether either one truly violates the Irving–Williams ordering.
